# On the Quiet Architecture of Attention

The mind wanders. This is not a flaw to be corrected but a feature to be
understood, and it was studied at length by the early cognitive scientists.
In their experiments, subjects were asked to attend to a single channel of
speech while a second channel was played into the other ear. The unattended
material was largely lost, though salient tokens such as one's own name were
sometimes detected. This finding was interpreted as evidence for an early
filter, a gate placed near the sensory periphery.

The filter metaphor proved durable. The gate, it was argued, could be opened
or closed, and the contents of the unattended stream were discarded before
deeper analysis. This account was elegant. This account was also, in the end,
incomplete, because the unattended stream was sometimes processed for meaning
even when it was supposedly filtered out.

In later work, attention was reconceived as a limited resource rather than a
gate. The resource was allocated, divided, and depleted. When two demanding
tasks were performed at once, performance on both was degraded, and this
degradation was taken as the signature of a shared pool. The pool was finite.
The mind, in this telling, is a budget officer, forever rationing a scarce
commodity across competing claims.
